author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Our Ignorance of Ourselves Illustrated by the Remarkable Memory of One Simplicius.
title: Our Ignorance of Ourselves Illustrated by the Remarkable Memory of One Simplicius.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Chapter 9 [VII.]—Our Ignorance of Ourselves Illustrated by the Remarkable Memory of One Simplicius.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Observe now, while we are, while we live, while we know that we live, while we are certain that we possess memory, understanding, and will; who boast of ourselves as having a great knowledge of our own nature;—observe, I say, how entirely ignorant we are of what avail to us is our memory, or our understanding, or our will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: A certain man who from his youth has been a friend of mine, named Simplicius, is a person of accurate and astonishing memory.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: I once asked him to tell me what were the last lines but one of all the books of Virgil; he immediately answered my question without the least hesitation, and with perfect accuracy.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: I then asked him to repeat the preceding lines; he did so.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And I really believe that he could have repeated Virgil line after line backward.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For wherever I wished, I made trial whether he could do it, and he did it.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Similarly in prose, from any of Cicero’s orations, which he had learnt by heart, he would perform a similar feat at our request, by reciting backwards as far as we wished.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Upon our expressing astonishment, he called God to witness that he had no idea of this ability of his previous to that trial.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: So far, therefore, as memory is concerned, his mind only then learnt its own power; and such discovery would at no time be possible except by trial and experiment.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Moreover, he was of course the very same man before he tried his powers; how was it, then, that he was ignorant of himself?
